variables.name: Area element
variables.note: Points outward.

variables.name: Enclosed charge
variables.note: Only charges inside.

formula_sheet.common_error: Plugging external charges into the enclosed charge term.

ncert_golden_lines: Gauss's law is true for any closed surface, no matter what its shape or size.
The term q on the right side of Gauss's law includes the sum of all charges enclosed by the surface.
The electric field E on the left side of the equation is due to all the charges, both inside and outside the surface.
The Gaussian surface can pass through a continuous charge distribution.

notes.title: Field due to an Infinitely Long Straight Uniformly Charged Wire
points: An infinitely long straight wire has a uniform linear charge density \lambda.
By symmetry, the electric field is everywhere radial in the plane cutting the wire normally, and its magnitude depends only on the radial distance r from the wire.
The direction of the electric field is radially outward for positive \lambda and radially inward for negative \lambda.
To calculate the field using Gauss's Law, a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length L, coaxial with the line charge, is chosen.
The electric field magnitude falls off as \frac{1}{r}, which is slower than the $\frac{1}{r^{2}}$ fall-off of a point charge.
notes.why_it_matters: Asked routinely as a direct 3-mark derivation and as a 2-mark graph drawing question (E vs r).

notes.statement: The electric field due to an infinite straight charged wire is inversely proportional to the perpendicular distance from the wire.

variables.name: Radial unit vector in the plane normal to the wire
variables.note: Points outward for +\lambda, inward for -\lambda.

notes.valid_when: Valid for infinitely long wires, or at distances r much smaller than the length of a finite wire (ignoring edge effects).
proof_skeleton: Consider an infinite wire with uniform linear charge density \lambda.
Construct a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length L coaxial with the wire.
Total flux \Phi = Flux through curved surface + Flux through two flat circular caps.
For flat caps, \vec{E} and d\vec{A} are perpendicular ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$), so flux is zero.
For curved surface, \vec{E} and d\vec{A} are parallel ($\theta = 0^{\circ}$), and E is constant. Flux = E \cdot (2\pi r L).
Charge enclosed by the cylinder is $q_{enc} = \lambda L$.
Apply Gauss's Law: $E(2\pi r L) = \frac{\lambda L}{\epsilon_{0}}$.
Cancel L and solve for E to get the final formula.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to an Infinitely Long Straight Uniformly Charged Wire
question_forms: Using Gauss's law, derive the expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged infinite straight wire. (3 marks)
Plot a graph showing the variation of electric field E with perpendicular distance r from an infinitely long straight charged wire. (2 marks)
board_traps: Students forget to mention why the flux through the flat circular caps of the Gaussian cylinder is zero. Without stating $\vec{E} \perp d\vec{A}$ (or $\theta = 90^{\circ}$), marks are deducted.
Drawing a straight line for the E vs r graph. It must be a rectangular hyperbola curve.
common_mistakes: Cancelling r instead of L during the derivation steps.
Writing the final formula with 4\pi$\epsilon_{0}$ instead of 2\pi$\epsilon_{0}$ in the denominator.
notes.answer_structure: Draw the wire and the coaxial Gaussian cylinder. Label r and L. Explicitly write the flux equations for all three surfaces (curved + top cap + bottom cap). Show caps are zero. Apply Gauss's Law, equate, cancel L, state final vector form.
notes.examiner_expectation: The examiner looks for a clearly labeled diagram of the Gaussian cylinder, the splitting of the integral into three parts, and the explicit cancellation of the arbitrary length L.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to an Infinitely Long Straight Uniformly Charged Wire
notes.one_liner: For a line charge, E falls off as $1/r$ and relies on a cylindrical Gaussian surface.
notes.memory_hook: Line = Cylinder = $1/r$. (Compare: Point = Sphere = $1/r^2$).
never_forget: E \propto \frac{1}{r} for a line charge.
The formula has 2\pi in the denominator, not 4\pi.
Flux through the flat ends of the cylinder is exactly zero.

notes.title: Gaussian Surface for a Line Charge

description.what_is_shown: An infinite straight charged wire surrounded by a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface.
elements: Vertical infinite line with positive charges (+)
A transparent cylinder of radius r and length l wrapping a section of the wire
Electric field vectors \vec{E} pointing radially outward, piercing the curved surface
Area vector \Delta\vec{S} on the curved surface parallel to \vec{E}
Flat circular end caps where \vec{E} is parallel to the surface (perpendicular to area vector)
description.spatial_relationships: The wire lies exactly on the central axis of the cylinder. \vec{E} vectors only cross the curved vertical walls, never the top or bottom circular caps.
description.style_notes: Dark background #0a0a0f. White lines and text. Cyan for electric field vectors. Yellow for area vectors.

notes.caption: Cylindrical Gaussian surface for an infinitely long straight wire.
notes.generation_prompt: Draw a 2D physics diagram of a vertical infinite straight wire. Mark positive plus signs along the wire. Draw a semi-transparent vertical cylinder centered on this wire. Label the cylinder's radius as r and its height as l. On the curved side of the cylinder, draw a cyan arrow pointing radially outward horizontally, labeled E. Next to it, draw a yellow arrow pointing in the exact same direction, labeled delta S. At the top flat circular cap, show that the electric field E is horizontal while the outward normal would be vertical. Use a solid dark #0a0a0f background.

notes.title: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Infinite Plane Sheet
points: An infinite plane sheet has a uniform surface charge density \sigma.
By symmetry, the electric field points outward and normal to the sheet for positive \sigma, and inward and normal for negative \sigma.
The magnitude of the electric field is strictly independent of the distance from the sheet.
To calculate the field, a rectangular or cylindrical 'pillbox' Gaussian surface is chosen, piercing the sheet normally.
The electric field is uniform in the entire space on either side of the infinite sheet.
notes.why_it_matters: Asked as a 3-mark derivation. More importantly, it forms the entire theoretical basis for the parallel plate capacitor in Chapter 2, making superposition of two sheets a high-frequency application question.

notes.statement: The electric field due to an infinite uniformly charged plane sheet is uniform and independent of distance.

variables.name: Unit vector normal to the plane
variables.note: Points away from the sheet.

notes.valid_when: Valid for infinite sheets, or at points close to the center of a finite sheet where edge effects are negligible.
proof_skeleton: Consider an infinite plane sheet with uniform surface charge density \sigma.
Construct a cylindrical/rectangular pillbox Gaussian surface of cross-sectional area A, piercing through the sheet, extending distance r on both sides.
Total flux \Phi = Flux through curved part + Flux through two flat ends.
For the curved part, \vec{E} is perpendicular to d\vec{A} ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$), so flux is zero.
For the two flat ends, \vec{E} is parallel to d\vec{A}. Flux = EA + EA = 2EA.
Charge enclosed by the pillbox is $q_{enc} = \sigma A$.
Apply Gauss's Law: $2EA = \frac{\sigma A}{\epsilon_{0}}$.
Cancel A and solve for E.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Infinite Plane Sheet
question_forms: Derive an expression for the electric field due to an infinite plane sheet of charge. (3 marks)
Two large parallel thin metallic plates have surface charge densities +\sigma and -\sigma. Find the electric field in the outer regions and in the inner region between the plates. (3 marks)
How does the electric field due to an infinite uniformly charged sheet vary with distance? (1 mark)
board_traps: Students assume the field must depend on distance 'r' and artificially introduce it into the formula. It is completely independent of distance.

When finding the field between two parallel sheets with opposite charges, students subtract the fields (getting 0) instead of adding them (getting $\sigma/\epsilon_{0}$). Directions must be checked carefully.
common_mistakes: Writing the final formula as \frac{\sigma}{$\epsilon_{0}$} instead of \frac{\sigma}{2$\epsilon_{0}$} for a single thin sheet.
Only calculating the flux through ONE flat end of the pillbox instead of both, losing the factor of 2.
notes.answer_structure: For derivation: draw sheet with piercing pillbox. State curved flux is zero. State flat flux is 2EA. Equate to \sigma A / $\epsilon_{0}$. Cancel A. For superposition problems: Draw the sheets. Pick a point. Draw E1 and E2 vectors based on charge signs. Add or subtract magnitudes based on vector directions.
notes.examiner_expectation: The clear statement that flux escapes through BOTH flat ends equally is required. The cancellation of the Area 'A' must be shown to prove distance/size independence.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Infinite Plane Sheet
notes.purpose: Demonstrate the superposition of electric fields from two parallel charged sheets (Capacitor setup).
notes.situation: Two large, thin, parallel metallic plates are placed close to each other. They have surface charge densities of +$17.7 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C/m}^{2}$ and $-17.7 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C/m}^{2}$ respectively. Calculate the electric field in the outer region of the first plate, and in the inner region between the plates.

steps.action: Determine the magnitude of the field produced by each individual sheet.
steps.why: The magnitude of the field from any single thin sheet is $\sigma/2\epsilon_{0}$, regardless of position.

steps.action: Analyze the outer region (Region I, to the left of the positive plate).
steps.why: In the outer region, the field from the positive plate points left, and the field from the negative plate points right. Being equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, they cancel exactly.

steps.action: Analyze the inner region between the plates (Region II).
steps.why: Between the plates, the field from the positive plate points right (away from it), and the field from the negative plate also points right (towards it). Since they point in the same direction, their magnitudes add up.

steps.action: Calculate the net field in the inner region.
steps.why: Adding the two halves gives the total uniform field.

steps.action: Substitute the numerical values.
steps.why: Final arithmetic step using the given permittivity value.

final_answer.direction: Inner field points from the positive to the negative plate.

notes.trap: Plugging the negative sign of $\sigma_2$ into the magnitude formula and algebraically subtracting them in the middle region to get zero. Always use absolute magnitudes and determine addition/subtraction visually from vector arrows.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Infinite Plane Sheet
notes.one_liner: An infinite sheet creates a uniform electric field $\sigma / 2\epsilon_{0}$ that does not fade with distance.
notes.memory_hook: A Sheet is like a Wall of Light. It doesn't get dimmer as you walk away. E is CONSTANT.
never_forget: E does NOT depend on 'r'.
Single thin sheet = $\sigma / 2\epsilon_{0}$.
Between two oppositely charged plates = $\sigma / \epsilon_{0}$.
Outside two oppositely charged plates = 0.

notes.title: Gaussian Surface for a Plane Sheet

description.what_is_shown: A flat uniformly charged infinite sheet pierced by a rectangular parallelepiped (pillbox) Gaussian surface.
elements: A planar 2D sheet with positive charge (+)
A transparent rectangular box piercing the sheet symmetrically
Cross-sectional area A marked on the flat end caps
Electric field vectors \vec{E} emerging horizontally outward from both flat end caps
Area vectors also emerging horizontally outward from both flat end caps
description.spatial_relationships: The electric field only pierces the two flat ends (labeled 1 and 2). It runs parallel to the top, bottom, and side faces of the pillbox, resulting in zero flux through those faces.
description.style_notes: Dark background #0a0a0f. White lines and text. Light blue plane. Cyan field vectors. Yellow area vectors.

notes.caption: Gaussian pillbox for an infinite plane sheet of charge.
notes.generation_prompt: Draw a 2D physics diagram of a large vertical plane sheet colored light blue, marked with positive plus signs. Draw a transparent rectangular box (a pillbox) horizontally piercing right through the middle of the sheet. Label the flat left end cap as '1' and the flat right end cap as '2'. From both end caps 1 and 2, draw cyan vector arrows pointing horizontally outward, away from the sheet, labeled E. Show that the area A of the caps is perpendicular to the electric field. Use a solid dark #0a0a0f background.

notes.title: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Thin Spherical Shell
points: A thin spherical shell of radius R has a uniform surface charge density \sigma, making total charge $q = 4\pi R^{2} \sigma$.
The charge resides entirely on the outer surface of the shell.
For points outside the shell (r > R), the electric field is identical to that of a point charge q concentrated entirely at the center.
For points on the surface of the shell (r = R), the electric field is maximum and equals $\frac{q}{4\pi\epsilon_{0}R^{2}}$.
For all points strictly inside the shell (r < R), the electric field is exactly zero. This is a crucial property related to electrostatic shielding.
notes.why_it_matters: The ultimate 5-mark Gauss's law question. Tests derivation across three different regions and requires combining them into a final discontinuous graph.

notes.statement: The electric field is zero inside a uniform spherical shell, and behaves like a point charge outside.

variables.name: Total charge on the shell
variables.note: $q = 4\pi R^{2}\sigma$

variables.name: Distance from the center of the shell

notes.valid_when: Strictly for a thin spherical shell with uniform charge distribution.
proof_skeleton: Case 1: Outside (r > R). Construct a spherical Gaussian surface of radius r concentric with the shell.
By symmetry, \vec{E} is radial, so \vec{E} \parallel d\vec{A} and E is constant. $\oint E dA = E(4\pi r^{2})$.
Charge enclosed is the total charge q.
Gauss's Law: $E(4\pi r^{2}) = \frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}$. Solve for E.
Case 2: Inside (r < R). Construct a spherical Gaussian surface of radius r concentric with the shell.
The flux is still E(4\pi $r^{2}$).
Charge enclosed is 0 (all charge is on the shell at radius R).
Gauss's Law: $E(4\pi r^{2}) = 0 \implies E = 0$.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Thin Spherical Shell
marks_breakdown: 5 marks (Full derivation for both regions + graph)
question_forms: Use Gauss's law to derive the expression for the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell at a point (i) outside and (ii) inside the shell. Draw a graph showing the variation of electric field with distance r from the centre. (5 marks)
Why is the electric field zero inside a charged spherical shell? (1 mark)
board_traps: Students try to do a volume integral or use volume charge density \rho. A thin shell only has surface charge density \sigma.
Drawing the E vs r graph as a continuous smooth curve. It must show a sudden discontinuity (jump from 0 to max) exactly at r = R.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'r' (variable distance) and 'R' (fixed radius) in the formulas.
Forgetting to draw the graph when asked, immediately losing 1 or 2 marks out of 5.
notes.answer_structure: Start with a diagram of the shell (radius R) and two dotted Gaussian spheres (r<R and r>R). Write Heading '1. Outside (r>R)', do the 4 steps. Write Heading '2. Inside (r<R)', do the 3 steps, clearly state $q_enc = 0$. Conclude with a clean E vs r graph.
notes.examiner_expectation: The explicit statement that $q_{enc}$ = 0 for the inside case is heavily weighted. The graph must clearly label the x-axis discontinuity point as r = R.

notes.concept_ref: Field due to a Uniformly Charged Thin Spherical Shell
notes.one_liner: A spherical shell hides all electric field inside itself (E=0), but acts exactly like a concentrated point charge to everything outside.
notes.memory_hook: Inside: Empty Space = Zero Field. Outside: Looks like a Point Charge.
never_forget: $E = 0$ for ALL points where r < R.
Graph starts flat on the zero line, spikes up at r = R, then curves down as $1/r^2$.
Maximum electric field exists exactly on the surface.

notes.title: Electric Field vs Distance for Spherical Shell

description.what_is_shown: A graph showing how electric field magnitude E varies with radial distance r for a thin charged spherical shell.
elements: Y-axis labeled E (Electric Field)
X-axis labeled r (Distance from center)
A vertical dashed line marking r = R (the surface of the shell)
A flat horizontal line running exactly on the x-axis from r = 0 to r = R (representing $E = 0$ inside)
A sudden jump at r = R up to a maximum value $E_{max} = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_{0}}\frac{q}{R^{2}}$
A curved line decaying from E_{max} towards the right for r > R, representing the $1/r^2$ fall-off.
description.spatial_relationships: The discontinuity at r = R is the most critical feature. The curve for r > R must approach the x-axis asymptotically but never touch it.
description.style_notes: Dark background #0a0a0f. White axes and text. Cyan line for the graph curve to make it pop.

notes.caption: Variation of electric field E with distance r for a thin spherical shell.
notes.generation_prompt: Draw a 2D line graph. The vertical y-axis is labeled E. The horizontal x-axis is labeled r. Mark a point R on the x-axis and draw a vertical dashed line up from it. From r=0 to r=R, draw a thick cyan line exactly flat on the x-axis, labeled E=0. At r=R, jump straight up to a high point on the y-axis. From this high point, draw a thick cyan curve sweeping downwards to the right, approaching the x-axis asymptotically. Label this curve E proportional to $1/r^2$. Use a solid dark #0a0a0f background with bright white axes and text.

ncert_golden_lines: The electric field due to an infinite plane sheet of charge is independent of the distance from the sheet.
For points outside the shell, the field is as if the entire charge of the shell is concentrated at its centre.
The electric field is zero at all points inside a uniformly charged thin spherical shell.
